content: Covered entities and business associates should limit uses and disclosures of protected health information to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose. When providing wellness support, collect only information required for the immediate interaction and avoid requesting full medical histories or identifiers unless operationally required and authorized.

Individuals generally have rights to access, amend, and receive an accounting of certain disclosures of their protected health information, subject to regulatory exceptions. Wellness agents should explain that conversational content may be sensitive and should not be stored beyond policy without appropriate safeguards and lawful basis.

Personal data concerning health is a special category under Article 9. Processing generally requires an explicit legal basis and often explicit consent or another Article 9(2) condition. Wellness coaching systems should minimize health data collection, document lawful basis, and avoid secondary uses without a valid basis.

Personal data must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed. Purpose limitation requires collecting data for specified, explicit, legitimate purposes and not further processing incompatibly with those purposes.

Health-related information requires heightened protection under UK GDPR. Services offering wellbeing support should perform appropriate risk assessments, provide clear privacy information, and ensure users understand when content is not clinical care.

Psychoeducation helps people understand stress responses and build coping skills such as sleep hygiene, physical activity, social connection, and structured problem-solving. These approaches support wellbeing but do not replace professional assessment when symptoms are severe, persistent, or impairing daily function.

Evidence-informed stress management includes identifying stressors, pacing workload where possible, brief relaxation or breathing exercises, regular breaks, and seeking supervisor or occupational support when workplace factors contribute to distress. Encourage professional help when stress is overwhelming or accompanied by hopelessness or self-harm thoughts.

source_doc: NHS Every Mind Matters / anxiety self-help (summary)
content: For mild to moderate anxiety, structured self-help can include grounding techniques (5-4-3-2-1 senses), paced breathing, limiting caffeine, maintaining routine, and talking to someone trusted. Seek GP or urgent care if anxiety is severe, includes panic attacks with chest pain, or involves thoughts of self-harm.

In the United States, dial or text 988 for the Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. For immediate danger, call 911. Do not delay emergency contact when someone may act on suicidal thoughts or is in immediate danger.
In the UK and Ireland, Samaritans can be reached at 116 123 for confidential emotional support. For immediate danger, call 999. Crisis support complements but does not replace emergency services.

Across many EU member states, 112 connects to emergency services. National crisis lines vary by country. When self-harm or imminent danger is disclosed, prioritize immediate in-person emergency support over coaching.

Wellness coaching may discuss emotions, stress, habits, motivation, and general coping strategies. It must not provide medical diagnosis, medication advice, psychotherapy treatment plans, or legal determinations. When users request clinical actions, redirect to qualified professionals while offering non-clinical support where appropriate.

A basic cognitive awareness exercise invites noticing a distressing thought, identifying the situation, rating intensity, and gently examining evidence for and against the thought. This is educational and not a formal CBT treatment protocol. Recommend licensed therapy for trauma, severe depression, or persistent intrusive thoughts.

General sleep hygiene includes consistent wake times, limiting screens before bed, reducing caffeine late in the day, a dark and cool sleep environment, and winding down with a brief relaxation routine. Persistent insomnia or sleep disruption with mood changes warrants professional evaluation.

A brief mindfulness exercise can guide attention to breath or bodily sensations for one to three minutes, noting distractions without judgment and returning focus gently. Mindfulness supports stress reduction for many people but may not suit active trauma responses; suggest professional guidance for trauma-related symptoms.
